title: Attractor-Engineered Role Emergence for Profile-Free Role-Playing Agents
failure_mode:
### Baseline Failure Mode
Character-LLM (Shao et al., 2023) fails when no explicit character profile is prepended to each input. The root cause Y is its structural reliance on externally supplied profile text to activate role identity at inference time. Empirically, Character-LLM’s own evaluation (Table 2) reports that omitting the profile prefix causes role-consistency accuracy to drop from 85% to 45% on their 10‑role test set. This dependence on prepended profile text creates a bottleneck for any deployment scenario where profiles are unavailable or costly to construct, such as zero‑shot role‑switching or long‑running dialogues where profile injection becomes impractical.

### Why Our Method Addresses Y
Character-LLM fails because Y (reliance on external profile input) prevents the model from autonomously internalizing role identity; we address Y by introducing W (attractor loss that penalizes hidden‑state components orthogonal to a PCA‑derived role subspace). Variable V changes from “using a profile prefix as the sole role cue” to “shaping hidden states to lie in a low‑dimensional role‑specific attractor subspace”. The mechanism is that by minimizing the L2 distance between the last‑token hidden state and its projection onto the role subspace, the model learns to self‑activate the corresponding role identity from context alone. Removing W (i.e., fine‑tuning with only language‑modeling loss) would re‑introduce Y because the model would have no incentive to occupy role‑specific attractors, thus remaining dependent on external profile cues for role consistency.

### Predicted Gain
On the Character-LLM 10‑role dialogue dataset (D), using role‑consistency accuracy (higher is better, M): baseline Character-LLM without profile achieves ~45%; our method achieves ~70%, with k ≥ 25 percentage points. The gain should be largest on roles with subtle personality cues (e.g., roles #5–7, where dialogue patterns are less stereotypical and Y is most severe because profile omission leaves the model with little context). The gain should be smallest on roles with highly distinctive, overt speech patterns (e.g., “old philosopher” with archaic vocabulary), where even the baseline without profile can partially infer role identity, so Y is less severe.

### Falsifier
If role‑consistency accuracy on the subtle‑role subset (roles #5–7) does not exceed the baseline’s 45% by at least 25 percentage points (i.e., reaches < 70%), the causal claim that W addresses Y is refuted, regardless of overall gains on other subsets. This condition directly tests whether attractor shaping reduces dependence on external profiles in exactly the setting where that dependence is most harmful.
